coredata.dc:title: A self-assembly hydrophobic oCDs/Ag nanoparticles SERS sensor for ultrasensitive melamine detection in milk
coredata.dc:creator: Qiu, Jiaoyan
Chu, Yujin
He, Qihang
Han, Yingkuan
Zhang, Yu
Han, Lin
coredata.dc:description: A simple, ultra-sensitive, and super-stable hydrophobic SERS platform for detection of melamine in milk is developed. The hydrophobic SERS platform was constructed via directly growing hydrophobic carbon/silver nanoparticles on glass by in-situ one-step carbonization using hexadecylpyridinium chloride monohydrate as stabilizer and reducing agent. The performances of SERS platform are systematically studied by using Rhodamine 6G (R6G) as a model, which achieves detection level of 10−13 M and enhancement factor of 3.4 × 1010 for R6G detection with good uniformity and reproducibility, as well as 110 days stability in air. The FDTD simulation was used to confirm SERS enhancement mechanism. More importantly, SERS platform delivers good linear property in the range from 0.01 to 100 ppm, and low limit detection of 9 ppb for melamine detection in milk through direct drop on the platform. The SERS platform could have great applications in food safety, environmental monitoring, biomedicine and other fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SERS
Melamine
High stability
Ultrasensitivity
Milk detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The fabrication and detection process of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor
Construction and characterization of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor
The sensitivity and uniformity of the SERS sensor
Stability and reproducibility of the SERS sensor
Melamine detection in water by the SERS sensor
Melamine detection in milk by the SERS sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62174101), the Major Scientific and Technological Innovation Project of Shandong Province (2021CXGC010603), the Fundamental Research Funds of Shandong University (2020QNQT001), We would like to thank Haiyan Yu, Xiaomin Zhao, Sen Wang and Yuyu Guo from State Key laboratory of Microbial Technology of Shandong University for help and guidance in material characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of preparation and malemine detection process of oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor.

Characterizations of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrates. (a) Hydrophilic and hydrophobic images of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrates with different growth times; (b) Raman spectra of R6G with concentration (10-7 M) on oCDs/Ag NPs @glass SERS substrates; (c) SERS intensity at 613 cm−1 , NPs size and NPs coverage area on the oCDs/Ag NPs with different growth time (inset of (c): photographs of the oCDs/Ag NPs @glass SERS substrates); (d) Low magnification SEM images (inset of (d): the high magnification),(e) Size distribution of oCDs/Ag NPs calculated by Nano Measurer, and (f) EDS spectra of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass substrate with the optimal synthesis conditions, respectively. (inset of (f): the Elemental mapping of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate).

The electric field distribution of the hydrophobic oCDs/Ag NPs with different size on the SERS substrate by FDTD simulation. 13.8 nm, 2 h (a); 18.9 nm, 4 h (b); 25.2 nm, 6 h (c); 28.15 nm, 8 h (d); 33.85 nm, 10 h (e); 39.85 nm, 12 h (f); 58.21 nm, 18 h (g) and (h) (E/E0)4 dependence on size of oCDs/Ag NPs with different growth time on the surface of glass.

(a) SERS spectra of R6G with different concentrations (10-13 M ∼ 10-6 M) on the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate (λex = 532 nm); (b) R6G concentrations dependent SERS intensity at the peak of 613 cm−1 on the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate (λex = 532 nm), (inset of (b): the linear relationship between SERS Intensity and log value of R6G concentrations (10-9 M ∼ 10-13 M)); (c) the FDTD simulation electrical field |E| distribution result by taking the SEM image of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate as the model under an excitation wavelength of 532 nm in x − y view (the color bar scales with E/E0); (d) Representative SERS intensity mapping of R6G on the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate, scanning area is 20 µm × 20 µm; (e) SERS spectra of R6G with concentration of 10-7 M on the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate and Raman spectra peak intensities of randomly selected 100 points on the SERS substrate.

(a) SERS spectra of R6G (10-6 M) measured on the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate with the different storage time in air and (b) storage time dependent relative SERS intensity of R6G (10-6 M) at the peak of 613 cm−1 on the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate and the Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate probability of stability and R6G with concentration (10-2 M) on the Ag NPs@glass, respectively; The SERS (c) spectra and (d) intensity at 613 cm−1 of R6G (10-7 M) on the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass substrates from the five different batches.

(a) The SERS spectra of different melamine concentrations (1 ppb-100 ppm) in water; (b) The linear relationship between SERS intensity and log value of melamine concentrations; (c) The SERS spectra of melamine (100 ppm) on the 5 different oCDs/Ag NPs@glass sensors; (d) The SERS intensity at 683 cm−1 of melamine (100 ppm) on the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor. The melamine in milk detection. (e) the milk pretreatment procedure; (f) SERS spectra of different concentrations melamine (0 ppm, 0.01 ppm, 0.1 ppm, 1 ppm, 10 ppm, 100 ppm) in milk, and (g) the concentrations dependent SERS signal intensity of melamine at the peak of 683 cm−1 on the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor (λex = 633 nm), respectively.

head.ce:title: A self-assembly hydrophobic oCDs/Ag nanoparticles SERS sensor for ultrasensitive melamine detection in milk

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Marine Science and Technology, Shandong University, Qingdao 266237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Marine Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Marine Science and Technology, Shandong University, Qingdao 266237, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we developed a novel, large area, hydrophobic oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor via one step in-situ self-assembly to detect the melamine in milk. The SERS sensor exhibits the ultra sensitivity with enhancement factor of 3.4×1010 and super-stability of storage in air for 110 days, and could detect melamine in 2 µL milk with LOD of 0.009 ppm. The SERS sensor could have important application value in the fields of food safety, environmental monitoring, and biomedicine.

A simple, ultra-sensitive, and super-stable hydrophobic SERS platform for detection of melamine in milk is developed. The hydrophobic SERS platform was constructed via directly growing hydrophobic carbon/silver nanoparticles on glass by in-situ one-step carbonization using hexadecylpyridinium chloride monohydrate as stabilizer and reducing agent. The performances of SERS platform are systematically studied by using Rhodamine 6G (R6G) as a model, which achieves detection level of 10−13 M and enhancement factor of 3.4 × 1010 for R6G detection with good uniformity and reproducibility, as well as 110 days stability in air. The FDTD simulation was used to confirm SERS enhancement mechanism. More importantly, SERS platform delivers good linear property in the range from 0.01 to 100 ppm, and low limit detection of 9 ppb for melamine detection in milk through direct drop on the platform. The SERS platform could have great applications in food safety, environmental monitoring, biomedicine and other fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, due to the frequent occurrence of poisoning, environmental safety has become an urgent concern (García-Miguel et al., 2018). Melamine (1,3,5-triazine-2,4,6-triamine) is rich in nitrogen, an organic compound that is often illegally added to dairy products to boost protein levels. It is well known that infants and animals ingesting with high concentrations of melamine can lead to pathological changes in the reproductive system or kidneys, and even death in some cases (Wang et al., 2016). The World Health Organization (WHO) and the US Food and Drug Administration (USFDA) have clearly stipulated that the melamine content in infant formula milk powder should not be higher than 1.0 mg·kg−1, and in liquid dairy products and other foods should be less than 2.5 mg·kg−1 (Lu et al., 2015). Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple, ultra-sensitive, rapid and stable sensor to detect melamine.

In recent years, a variety of efficient methods have developed to accelerate the effective detection of melamine in food, such as gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (Li, Qi, & Shi, 2009), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Guedes-Alonso, Sosa-Ferrera, & Santana-Rodríguez, 2017), high performance liquid chromatography (Zhu et al., 2015), inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (Zhang et al., 2019), enzyme-linked immunoassay (ELISA) (Lu et al., 2017). Although these methods have the characteristics of rapid and accurate detection. Some limitations including expensive instrument, high cost of analysis, time consumption, cumbersome sample pretreatment and large consumption reagent restrained their application and development. In order to overcome the above limitations, some new sensors were developed including electrochemical sensors (Rahman & Ahmed, 2018), fluorescence (Barreto et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022), colorimetric (Jigyasa & Rajput, 2018; Karimi, Farrokhnia, & Askarian, 2019), Raman (Arabi et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2022). Especially, SERS has been widely applied in the fields of toxic substances, food safety and clinical diagnosis due to the fingerprint recognition function and ultra-sensitive molecular vibration mode. Currently, noble metal nanomaterials (e.g. Au, Ag, Cu) are widely used in the process of constructing SERS active substrates. The SERS signal of target molecules is enhanced through electromagnetic enhancement mechanism. For instance, Chen (J. Chen et al., 2016) et al. designed a flexible SERS-Enhanced substrate that dropped gold nanoparticles on commercial tape and applied to detect various pesticide residues. Li (Li et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022) developed a novel hydrophilic membrane and a SERS-imprinted composite membrane to detect l-tyrosine, which can broaden the application range of SERS detection. Wang (Wang et al., 2017) et al., developed a flexible substrate deposited silver nanoparticles on 3D PDMS nanotentacles array, which was ultimately used to detect pesticide residues. Our group also developed a dual-enhanced SERS sensor for ultrasensitive lead (II) detection based on graphene and hybrid nanostructures (He et al., 2021). However, the stability and sensitivity of the SERS still need to be improved. The construction of a hydrophobic SERS substrate has attracted wide attention (Chen et al., 2016), which can gather a large number of target molecules on the hot spots of SERS substrate for enhancing SERS signal (Sakir, Yilmaz, & Onses, 2020; Wang et al., 2021). However, the preparation of super-hydrophobic and stable SERS substrates was normally fabricated through a variety of nano-manufacturing equipment, electron beam photolithography, optical photolithography and other processes, resulting in the high cost for practical application (Zhang et al., 2019). Therefore, it’s necessary to develop a stable, ultrasensitive and large-area SERS substrate via a simple procedure for the harmful substances detection.

In this work, we developed a novel hydrophobic SERS platform with large area, high stability and ultrasensitivity for the quantitative detection of the melamine in the milk. A hydrophobic carbon/silver nanoparticles (oCDs/Ag NPs)@glass SERS sensor was constructed by directly growing oCDs/Ag NPs on clean glass via in situ one-step carbonization using hexadecylpyridinium chloride monohydrate (CPC) as stabilizer and reducing agent. The performances of the SERS platform are systematically studied. It achieves low limit detection (LOD) of 10-13 M and SERS enhancement factor of 3.4 × 1010 for R6G detection, as well as 110 days stability in air. The finite difference time domain (FDTD) simulation confirmed the SERS signal enhancement mechanism. More importantly, the SERS platform was applied in the melamine detection in water and milk, which exhibits a good linear property in the range from 0.01 ppm to 100 ppm, and LOD of 9 ppb.

Melamine, Cysteine (Cys), Glycine (Gly), Vitamin C (Vc) and Glucose (Glu) were ordered from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). CPC, ethanol, silver nitrate (AgNO3), rhodamine 6G (R6G), NaCl, KCl, MgSO4 and Ca(NO3)2 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was ordered from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). The glass was purchased from Citotest (Jiangsu, China). Pure milk was bought by Jingdong stores (Beijing, China). All the chemical reagents and solvent in the whole experiment were analytical grade or above and Deionized water of 18.2 MΩ*cm was obtained by using a filter device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fabrication and detection process of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The specific fabrication and melamine detection process of oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor was displayed in Fig. 1 . The oCDs/Ag NPs nanomaterials were synthesized by one step self-assembly in the solution. The specific synthesis steps are as follows: firstly, AgNO3 (5 mL, 45 mM) was added into CPC (5 mL, 15 mM) solution, after stirring for 30 min (400 rpm), the color of the solution was milky white. Secondly, NaOH (2 mL, 1 M) solution was added into the mixed solution. With the extension of time, the color of the mixture solution gradually changed from milky white to brown. Finally, the oCDs/Ag NPs nanomaterials were synthesized successfully. For in situ self-assembly hydrophobic oCDs/Ag NPs on glass substrate. Firstly, the bare glass slides were washed with ethanol and ultrasonic washed for 3 times, respectively. Then the glass slide was treated by the oxygen plasma. Secondly, the glass slide was placed into the mixture solution NaOH (2 mL, 1 M), CPC (5 mL, 15 mM) and AgNO3 (5 mL, 45 mM) for incubation 12 h followed by DI water rinsing and N2 blow dry. The oCDs/Ag NPs@glass substrate were synthesized successfully. During the experiment, the color of the mixture changed from milky white to dark brown due to the CPC molecules carbonized, which in situ generate oCDs on the around of Ag NPs to grow on the glass.

For melamine detection, the specific steps are as follows based on previous literature (Wen et al., 2021): firstly, the melamine solution was added into the milk solution to make-up a 200 ppm melamine milk solution, then adding HCl (1 M, 1 mL) into the spiked milk (25 mL), then centrifuged by Eppendorf centrifuge (9000 rpm, 5 min); secondly, the supernatant was collected and filtered with a 0.22 µm PTFE filter. Thirdly, The supernatant was diluted into different concentrations of 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10 and 100 ppm, respectively. The 2 µL of milk solutions with different concentrations of melamine were directly dropped on the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor. After drying, the melamine SERS spectra was collected (excitation wavelength: 633 nm, power: 10 %, scanning time: 1 s, rating of 1800 l/mm, objective length: about 50 × and laser power of 17 mW (10 % ×)).

The UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum of the material were recorded on a microplate using a 96-well quartz plate (Corning, Cat. No. 3595), and the range of wavelength was 400 nm-800 nm. SERS analysis of R6G and melamine was carried out with InVia Raman spectrometer. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of oCDS/Ag NPs@glass were obtained by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FEI Quanta250 FEG). The FDTD method was used to simulate the distribution of electromagnetic field of oCDs/Ag NPs@glass (the SEM image was imported into the system as the structure model, and the parameters were set to simulate the distribution of electromagnetic field intensity of the SERS substrate).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and characterization of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The construction and characterization of the hydrophobic oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate was depicted in Fig. 1. Hydrophobic oCDs/Ag NPs was synthesized in one step by mixing CPC and AgNO3 in the presence of NaOH. The synthesis of the Ag NPs, oCDs and oCDs/Ag NPs were confirmed. As displayed in Fig. S1, the UV–vis spectrum of Ag NPs, oCDs and oCDs/Ag NPs were compared. The Ag NPs, oCDs and oCDs/Ag NPs solutions appear color of light brown, yellowish and dark brown, respectively. The UV–vis spectra of oCDs without Ag NPs showed two obvious absorption peaks at 350 nm and 401 nm, which were corresponding to the Π – Π* and n – Π* transition of oCDs (Qu et al., 2014), indicating the carbonization of CPC in the presence of NaOH (Zheng et al., 2015). For the oCDs/Ag NPs have two similar peaks are appeared at 358 nm and 407 nm. The calculated absorbance ratio of the oCDs/Ag NPs at 407 and 358 nm (A407/A358) is much higher than that of the oCDs (A407/A358), indicating the successfully preparation of Ag NPs and oCDs/Ag NPs (Peng et al., 2019; Lei Wang et al., 2011). The fluorescence spectrum of the Ag NPs, oCDs and oCDs/Ag NPs solutions are further confirmed their successful synthesis as shown in Fig. S2. The two fluorescence peaks at 550 nm and 650 nm appear for the oCDs and oCDs/Ag NPs samples (Ding et al., 2016; Pinto et al., 2017). There is no any fluorescence peak for Ag NPs solution. The results confirmed that the oCDs/Ag NPs can be successfully synthesized with the simple strategy.

To obtain the best performances of the oCDs/Ag NPs SERS sensor, the oCDs/Ag NPs SERS substrates were prepared with different oCDs/Ag NPs growth time. The SEM images of oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate with different growth time as displayed in Fig. S3. With the growth time increase, the oCDs/Ag NPs appears to agglomerate onto the glass substrate, and the coverage area of oCDs/Ag NPs increases from 14 % to 79 %. The size distribution of oCDs/Ag NPs on the glass surface at the different growth time were calculated by Nano Measurer, which are from 13.8 nm to 58.21 nm, as shown in Fig. S4. To characterize the surface hydrophobic property of the oCDs/Ag NPs on the glass surface, the contact angles were measured on the oCDs/Ag NPs SERS active substrates with different growth time as shown in Fig. 2 (a). The contact angle gradually increases from 58˚ to 125˚ with the oCDs/Ag NPs growth time from 2 h to 18 h, respectively, proving that the prepared oCDs/Ag NPs SERS active substrate is hydrophobic. Fig. 2(b) reveals the Raman spectrum of R6G on oCDs/Ag NPs SERS active substrate with different growth time. To avoid the interference, the Raman spectrum of the oCDs/Ag NPs SERS active substrate was recorded as exhibited in Fig. S5, which shows the weak carbon Raman shift peak at 1600 cm−1. From Fig. 2(b), we can also see that the Raman intensity of R6G gradually increase with the oCDs/Ag NPs growth time within 12 h, and start to decrease when the growth time over 12 h. The R6G Raman intensity at 613 cm−1, surface coverage and oCDs/Ag NPs size and on the SERS active substrates with the different growth time are summarized in Fig. 2(c). It clearly exhibits that oCDs/Ag NPs SERS active substrate prepared with 12 h delivers the best performances. Therefore, we finally ensure the optimal growth time of 12 h for the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor.

The SEM images of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS active substrate prepared with 12 h are shown in Fig. 2(d), which shows the dense and uniform oCDs/Ag NPs film. The average of oCDs/Ag NPs size distribution was calculated by Nano Measurer is about 39.85 nm as displayed in Fig. 2(e). In order to further study the morphological structure of oCDs/Ag NPs, we conducted TEM performed on local nanocomposite particles and single nanocomposite particles. As shown in Fig. S6. TEM images showed that the size of oCDs/Ag NPs was distributed between 30 and 40 nm, which was consistent with SEM results. The EDS spectra and element images of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass substrate are shown in Fig. 2(f). By comparison with that of the bare glass in Fig. S7, the content of C and Ag in oCDs/Ag NPs@glass substrate in Table S1 was significantly higher than that of the bare glass, proving that the oCDs/Ag NPs nanomaterial was successfully synthesized on the substrate. With the optimized conditions, oCDs/Ag NPs substrate with the large area was successfully synthesized as shown in Fig. S8.

The mechanism of SERS enhancement mainly attributed to the local surface plasmons (LSPR) from rough surfaces of metal materials or metal nanoparticles generated strong electromagnetic fields. To understand the SERS mechanism of the oCDs/Ag NPs SERS substrate, we modeled and simulated local E-field of oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS active substrate through the FDTD method. A plane wave source of 532 nm was adopted, which polarized in the X direction and propagated along the Z direction. The intensity of incident field is E0 = 1 V/m. Fig. 3 (a-g) present the simulation results for the oCDs/Ag NPs SERS substrate with the growth time from 2 h to18 h. It can be seen that the electromagnetic field intensity increases gradually with the growth time, and reaches maximum with 12 h growth, then start to decrease. Based the above results, (E/E0)4 are calculated as displayed in Fig. 3(h), which indicates that the maximum electromagnetic enhancement in the simulation area (Han et al., 2021; Han et al., 2020). It displayed that the oCDs/Ag NPs substrate grown 12 h with the average particle size of ∼39.85 nm gives the best electromagnetic enhancement effect. The simulation results are consistent with the experimental results, proving that the prepared SERS substrate has excellent electromagnetic enhancement effect and is suitable for ultrasensitive target detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensitivity and uniformity of the SERS sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The R6G concentrations (10-13 M ∼ 10-6 M) dependent SERS spectra are viewed in Fig. 4 (a). We can clearly see that the characteristic peaks of R6G at 613, 774, 928, 1089, 1126, 1183, 1310, 1360, 1572, and 1651 cm−1 (Peng et al., 2019), even at the concentrations of 10-10-10-13 M (Fig. S9). To present the significant difference in Raman intensity at low concentrations of R6G, the Raman spectra and SERS intensity at 613 cm−1 of R6G from 10-10 M − 10-13 M were shown Fig. S9. The obvious differences of SERS intensity (Δ1407.49 - Δ4587.95) among different R6G concentrations (10-10 M − 10-13 M) to the background value (10.6) were observed clearly. Taking the SERS intensity at 613 cm−1 as an example, it can be seen that the Raman intensity at 613 cm−1 gradually increases with the increase of R6G concentration. The correlated linear relationship between peak intensity and concentration of R6G was obtained with coefficient (R2) of 0.997 for the concentrations from 10-13 M to 10-9 M, and 0.978 for the concentrations of R6G from 10-9 M to 10-6 M, respectively, as displayed in Fig. 4(b). The results proved that the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate has ultrasensitivity for the target direct detection.

To characterize the enhancement factor of the SERS platform, Raman spectrum of R6G (10-13 M) on oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor and 10-2 M R6G on blank glass were measured, respectively, as displayed in Fig. S10. The characteristic SERS peak at 613 cm−1 of R6G was chosen to calculate the SERS enhancement factor (EF) by following equation (Han et al., 2021):
EF =
I
SERS
I
NR
×
C
NR
C
SERS
wherein ISERS and INR represent the Raman intensity of R6G on oCDs/Ag NPs sensor and the bare glass substrate, respectively; CSERS and CNR represent the corresponding R6G concentration on oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor and blank glass substrate, respectively. The high enhancement factor of 3.4 × 1010 was obtained for the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate, which further proved the ultrasensitivity of the SERS sensor. The enhancement mechanism may be attributed to (1) the optimized NPs inter-distance enhanced Raman signals; it because that the hydrophobic oCDs/Ag NPs nanoparticles grown on the surface of the substrate are pretty uniform under the optimal experimental conditions so that the distance between the oCDs/Ag NPs could reach the optimal value of Raman enhancement (S et al., 2022); (2) the concentration effects of hydrophobic oCDs/Ag NPs nanoparticles. It because that the hydrophobic SERS substrate can aggregate the target molecules to the hot spot region of the substrate thus enhancing SERS signal (Han et al., 2022; Han et al., 2022).

To prove the excellent uniformity of the oCDs/Ag NPs SERS substrate, the FDTD simulation and Raman mapping were performed, as displayed in the Fig. 4(c-e). Fig. 4(c) showed the simulation result of the electromagnetic field intensity on the surface of SERS substrate, which showed the strong and uniform EM Raman enhancement. During simulation, the SEM image of SERS substrate grown at 12 h as the model were directly imported into FDTD. Fig. 4d showed the Raman mapping of R6G with 100 points was performed on the hydrophobic SERS active substrate. It showed RSD of 100 points in the Raman mapping is 6.19 %, which further proved the excellent uniformity of the oCDs/Ag NPs SERS substrate. Fig. 4e showed the Raman spectra of 100 points randomly picked from the Raman mapping. The above results verified that the developed hydrophobic oCDs/Ag NPs SERS substrate had good uniformity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reproducibility of the SERS sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The long-time stability and repeatability of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS active substrate was verified by measuring the Raman spectrum of 10-7 M R6G at the time of 1th ∼ 110th days, respectively. The Fig. 5 (a) displays the time dependent Raman spectrum of R6G on the oCDs/Ag NPs SERS active substrate. The Raman signal intensity of R6G just slightly decrease. Taking the SERS intensity of R6G characteristic peak of 613 cm−1 to determine the exact SERS platform degradation, as shown in Fig. 5(b), SERS intensity remains 92 % and 82 % of as prepared oCDs/Ag NPs SERS platform after storage in air for 30 and 60 days, respectively. The Raman intensity signal remained at 73 % when storage time reached 110 days, which indicating that the hydrophobic oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate had significant stability. While, the SERS intensity of Ag substrate dropped to 60 % within 1 week and below 50 % (16 % after 3 weeks due to the Ag degradation). The Fig. S11 shows the time dependent Raman spectra of R6G on the Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate. The results indicated that the oCDs/Ag NPs SERS substrate has excellent long-term stability in air. Two reasons are proposed to explain the ultra-stability of the oCDs/Ag NPs SERS substrate. One reason is that the hydrophobic property of the oCDs/Ag NPs prevent the water to contact Ag NPs directly and slowdown the Ag degradation. Another reason is that Ag NPs wrapped by the carbon dots, and oxidation moleculars cannot reach the Ag easily (Chen et al., 2015). To determine the reproducibility of the oCDs/Ag NPs SERS substrate, we randomly selected 5 different batches of oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate to measure the Raman spectrum of R6G on them. By dropping 2 µL 10-7 M R6G solution on each SERS substrate, as displayed in Fig. 5(c), it showed the almost same SERS spectra for the 5 different SERS substrate. In Fig. 5(d) expressed the Raman intensity at 613 cm−1 of R6G, which gives a RSD of 5.44 % indicating the good repeatability of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate. The good stability and reproducibility of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate pave the way to the detection and analysis of actual samples.

In practical application, reusability is an important factor to reduce the cost, and can make use of sensors with quick analysis and portability (Doiron et al., 2019). At present, most SERS sensor can only be used for single sensor detection, with poor reusability and lack of stability (Pan et al., 2019). Therefore, SERS sensor with reusability has become a hotspot of current research, which helps to save a lot of manpower, material resources and provides a solution for environmental protection. The reusability SERS sensor may need to be conducted in the near future.

To investigate the selectivity of the proposed method for the detection of melamine in the milk, the potential interfering substances in the milk such as common ions (Na+, K+, Cl-, Ca2+, Mg2+, SO4 2-, NO3 –), Glycine (Gly), Vitamin C (Vc), Cysteine (Cys) and Glucose (Glu) were also investigated as presented in Fig. S12. The results showed a more intuitive evaluation of selectivity. It is clearly seen that even the concentration of the other interferences was 10 times higher than that of melamine, no melamine characteristic peak appeared at 683 cm−1, which was only observed in the presence of melamine. To further evaluate the cross-interaction of the interfering substances during melamine detection, the mixture solution of common ions and melamine was prepared and detected. The results showed that the characteristic peak at 683 cm−1 was observed in the mixture. But it is noteworthy that when all the interferences were mixed, the intensity at 683 cm−1 decreased slightly, which may be due to the reaction of some amino-containing and hydroxyl-containing the interfering substances. Thus, the results demonstrate that the proposed hydrophobic oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor shows a good specificity for melamine detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Melamine detection in water by the SERS sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, excitation laser wavelength for detecting the melamine was optimized as shown in Fig.S13. A strong melamine specific peak is present in the SERS spectra, located at 678–700 cm−1(Wen et al., 2021), which is the in-plane deformation mode of triazine ring in the molecular structure of melamine that the triazine ring is the unique structure of the melamine molecule (Huang, Cheng, & Lai, 2020). It shows that 633 nm is the suitable Raman excition laser wavelength for melamine detection, which gives the strongest Raman signals of melamine. Therefore, the 633 nm laser was selected to detect melamine. To verify whether the SERS substrate has the SERS enhanced effect on the melamine detection, 2 µL melamine solution (10-3 M) was dropped onto the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate and bare glass, respectively. As displayed in the Fig. S14, the characteristic peak appeared obviously at 683 cm−1 of melamine on the SERS substrate. There was no obvious melamine Raman signal on the blank glass. The results indicated that the SERS substrate could be used to sensitively detect melamine with strong SERS enhancement.

To detect the melamine in water using the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor, a series of melamine sparked in water solutions with different concentrations from 0.001 ppm to 100 ppm were prepared. The different concentration melamine solution was directly dropped onto the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS substrate, and each concentration was tested at least 3 times for obtaining the standard deviation. Fig. 6 (a) shows the concentration dependent SERS spectra of melamine. The characteristic peak of melamine was observed at 683 cm−1. The good linear relationship between Raman intensity at 683 cm-1and the melamine concentrations was obtained with R2 of 0.993, as displayed in Fig. 6(b). Moreover, the lowest detection concentration reaches 0.001 ppm, indicating that the SERS sensor based on oCDs/Ag NPs@glass has good sensitivity and LOD, and it may have potential applications in many fields.

To assess the reproducibility of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor for detecting melamine, we randomly selected 5 different batches of the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor. Melamine aqueous solution of 10-3 M was dropped onto the different oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensors, respectively. Fig. 6(c) displays the Raman spectra performed on the different SERS sensors, in which the almost same Raman intensity of melamine at 683 cm−1 appeared on different SERS sensors. Fig. 6(d) expresses the SERS intensity of melamine at 683 cm−1 on the different SERS sensors. and the RSD is only 2.32 %. The results proved that the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor has good repeatability for the melamine detection in water. The results imply that the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor has a excellent long term stability, reproducibility, and could be used for detection of melamine in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Melamine detection in milk by the SERS sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the melamine detection in practical samples, the melamine in raw milk was detected through direct drop the milk on the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor. Because milk samples contain many ingredients such as protein, sugar, fat and so on, the milk was pretreated before measurement, then the melamine with different concentrations from 0.01 to 100 ppm was prepared and detected. Firstly, we need to confirm whether the original milk contains the melamine by detecting the original milk sample based on the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor. The raw milk bought from the supermarket was directly dropped on the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor before adding melamine, the result is shown in Fig. S15. The Raman peak of melamine was not observed at the 683 cm−1 indicating no melamine in the original milk. Next, we pretreated the milk as shown in the Fig. 6(e), through centrifugation, extraction, direct drop sample, the Raman spectra of the different concentrations of melamine in milk were obtained. As displayed in Fig. 6(f), it can be viewed clearly that the lowest melamine concentration of 0.01 ppm can be detected with strong melamine characteristic peak of 683 cm−1. In the dynamic range from 0.01 ppm to 100 ppm, the good linear curve with R2 = 0.998 between the SERS intensity at 683 cm−1 and the melamine concentrations was achieved as shown in Fig. 6(g). The detection limits of melamine were calculated according to the formula, LOD = 3σ/S (Qiu et al., 2022), where S is the slope in the linear regression equation and σ is the standard deviation of the blank value. The equation for the standard deviation is.
σ =
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
(
x i
-
x
average
)
2
,

The LOD of 0.009 ppm was obtained, which is far below the safety limit of 2.5 ppm required by the United States and the European Union (Hu et al., 2019; H. Zhu et al., 2016). The results demonstrated that the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor would be good tool to detect melamine in milk rapidly and sensitively. In addition, by comparison with other detection methods as shown in the Table S2, the developed SERS sensor showed obvious advantages in terms of the dynamic detection range, LOD and enhancement factor. Therefore, the oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS sensor could be applied to detect melamine in real milk samples with excellent performance.

In summary, we developed a ultrasensitive, ultrastable and large-area oCDs/Ag NPs@glass SERS platform by one step in situ self-assembled oCDs/Ag NPs on the surface of glass for ultrasensitive melamine detection in milk by direct-drop method. The SERS substrate delivers improved SERS sensitivity, uniformity and stability, which attributed to the hydrophobic effect of oCDs/Ag NPs induced more target molecules adsorption and water repelling. The FDTD simulation confirmed the SERS signal enhancement mechanism. The LOD of 10-13 M and SERS enhancement factor of 3.4 × 1010 were reached in the process of R6G detection with good linear property, uniformity and reproducibility. More impressively, the SERS substrate could be still remaining over 80 % signals even instored in air for 60 days and maintain more than 73 % signals even instored in the air for 110 days. In the practical application of melamine detection in milk, the SERS sensor presents good linear Raman signal-to-concentration relationship in the dynamic range of 0.01 ∼ 100 ppm and the LOD down to 0.009 ppm. The developed SERS platform would have potential applications in food, chemistry environment, medicine and so on.

Jiaoyan Qiu: Investigation, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Yujin Chu: Investigation, Validation, Methodology, Supervision. Qihang He: Investigation, Software, Formal analysis. Yingkuan Han: Methodology. Yu Zhang: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Lin Han: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62174101), the Major Scientific and Technological Innovation Project of Shandong Province (2021CXGC010603), the Fundamental Research Funds of Shandong University (2020QNQT001), We would like to thank Haiyan Yu, Xiaomin Zhao, Sen Wang and Yuyu Guo from State Key laboratory of Microbial Technology of Shandong University for help and guidance in material characterization.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.134241.
